1: David’s sons born in Hebron: Amnon (firstborn, by Ahinoam), Daniel/Chileab (by Abigail), Absalom (by Maacah the Aramean princess), Adonijah (by Haggith), Shephatiah (by Abital), Ithream (by Eglah). None of these six sons became the ancestor of the Messianic line (the Messianic line goes through Solomon, born in Jerusalem). Amnon and Absalom died violently; Adonijah was executed by Solomon. The Hebron-period sons represent the fragility and violence within the Davidic house before Solomon’s consolidation — prefiguring the suffering that the Davidic dynasty endured before the eternal throne was secured.
2: The continuation of David’s Hebron-born sons (six in total). The six Hebron sons are succeeded by four Jerusalem sons of Bathsheba (v5) and nine more sons (vv6-8). David had seventeen sons listed here — but only one is in the Matthean genealogy (Solomon) and only one is in the Lukan genealogy (Nathan). The multiplicity of sons highlights the selectivity of the Messianic line: of seventeen sons, two carry the two genealogical traditions to Christ.
3: Absalom (son of Maacah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur) and Adonijah (son of Haggith). Absalom’s mother was an Aramean princess — another Gentile thread in the Davidic family. Absalom’s revolt against David and death (2 Sam 18) and Adonijah’s bid for the throne (1 Kgs 1-2) represent the covenant curse working within the Davidic house (2 Samuel 12:10: ‘the sword shall never depart from your house’) before the promised peaceful son (Solomon) established the kingdom.
4: Six sons were born to David in Hebron, where he reigned seven years and six months; and then he reigned thirty-three years in Jerusalem. The Hebron/Jerusalem division (7.5 + 33 = 40.5 total years) frames the Davidic reign as a progression from partial to full installation. David’s thirty-three years in Jerusalem — the number that would later be associated with the Messiah’s earthly lifespan — mark the fullness of the Davidic reign from the covenant city. The number 40 as the full biblical period (wilderness 40 years, Saul 40 years, David 40 years, Solomon 40 years) marks David’s reign as the paradigmatic full covenant generation.
5: Solomon was born in Jerusalem to Bathsheba (daughter of Ammiel, here called Bathshua). Also born to Bathsheba: Shimea, Shobab, and Nathan. Two critical genealogical facts converge here: (1) Solomon is the ancestor in Matthew’s genealogy (Matthew 1:6-7: ‘David was the father of Solomon by the wife of Uriah’) — Matthew names Bathsheba indirectly as ‘the wife of Uriah,’ the fourth woman in his genealogy, each representing a boundary-crossing in the Messianic line’s descent. (2) Nathan is the brother of Solomon through Bathsheba and the ancestor in Luke’s genealogy (Luke 3:31: ‘the son of Nathan, the son of David’). The two genealogical streams — Matthew through Solomon, Luke through Nathan — diverge at this verse. The Matthean stream carries the royal-legal line of succession; the Lukan stream (likely Mary’s biological line) carries the biological Davidic descent without passing through Jeconiah’s curse.
6: Nine more sons of David by other women: Ibhar, Elishua, Elpelet, Nogah, Nepheg, Japhia, Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet. These sons are also listed in 2 Samuel 5:14-16 (with minor variations in the Elishua/Elishama and Elpelet/Eliphelet pairs). None of these sons enters the Messianic genealogy. The breadth of the Davidic household — seventeen sons and one daughter (Tamar, v9) — shows the fullness of royal provision but also the danger: a large competing royal family was a political and personal liability, as Absalom’s revolt and Adonijah’s claim demonstrate.
7: The continuation of David’s nine Jerusalem-born sons (excluding the Bathsheba four). These sons form the full scope of David’s Jerusalem royal household. As David’s offspring, they share the covenant promise of 2 Samuel 7:12 (‘I will raise up your offspring after you’) in its broadest sense; the narrowing to Solomon for the dynastic succession and to Nathan for the biological line is a further divine selection within the already-selected Davidic household.
8: More of David’s sons and the summary of nine Jerusalem sons. The full Davidic offspring roster is now complete. Revelation 22:16 applies the Davidic lineage title to Christ in its fullest form: ‘I, Jesus, have sent my angel to testify to you about these things for the churches. I am the root and the descendant of David, the bright morning star.’ Both titles are held simultaneously: he is David’s root (pre-existent creator) and David’s descendant (covenant fulfiller).
9: These were all the sons of David (besides the sons of the concubines), and their sister Tamar. David’s daughter Tamar is named — the victim of Amnon’s assault (2 Sam 13), the crime that set in motion the chain of violence predicted in 2 Sam 12. The Chronicler preserves her name in the genealogical record. As with Tamar the daughter-in-law of Judah (1 Chronicles 2:4), a Tamar’s suffering is embedded in the Davidic genealogy — a reminder that the covenant line passed through human tragedy, not only triumph. The Messianic story includes the suffering of the innocent (Matthew 2:16-18, the slaughter of the innocents at Bethlehem) from the very beginning.
10: Solomon fathered Rehoboam; Rehoboam fathered Abijah; Abijah fathered Asa. These three are the first three post-Solomon kings in Matthew 1:7: ‘Solomon was the father of Rehoboam, and Rehoboam the father of Abijah, and Abijah the father of Asaph/Asa.’ Each king represents a generation of the covenant line between Solomon’s temple-building and the exile. Rehoboam’s kingdom split (1 Kgs 12); the Chronicler’s list preserves the Judahite throne-succession as the unbroken Davidic chain despite political catastrophe.
11: Asa fathered Jehoshaphat; Jehoshaphat fathered Joram; Joram fathered Ahaziah. Matthew 1:8 continues: ‘Asa the father of Jehoshaphat, and Jehoshaphat the father of Joram, and Joram the father of Uzziah.’ Matthew compresses three generations between Joram and Uzziah — Ahaziah, Joash, and Amaziah — for the 14-generation structure of his genealogy. The Chronicler preserves the complete list; Matthew’s selective omission follows the OT precedent of genealogical compression without falsifying the lineage.
12: Ahaziah (compressed out of Matthew’s genealogy) fathered Joash; Joash fathered Amaziah; Amaziah fathered Azariah/Uzziah. Uzziah is the king at whose death Isaiah received his throne-room vision (Isaiah 6:1) — the prophet of ‘a young woman will conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel’ (Isaiah 7:14, cited in Matthew 1:23). The Immanuel prophecy was given in the reign of Uzziah’s grandson Ahaz; the Davidic king whose name appears in the Isaiah-Immanuel context is traceable back through the genealogy to this verse.
13: Azariah/Uzziah fathered Jotham; Jotham fathered Ahaz; Ahaz fathered Hezekiah — Matthew 1:9-10 exactly. Ahaz is the faithless king who rejected the Immanuel sign (Isaiah 7:10-13) and sought Assyrian alliance instead of YHWH’s help. Yet the Messianic promise was given in his reign (Isaiah 7:14; 1 Chronicles 9:6-7) precisely as the counter to his faithlessness. Christ is the Immanuel who arrives even when (especially when) covenant faithfulness is absent in the royal line.
14: Hezekiah fathered Manasseh; Manasseh fathered Amon; Amon fathered Josiah — Matthew 1:10. Manasseh is the most notorious king in Chronicles (2 Chronicles 33:1-20): he filled Jerusalem with idols, shed innocent blood, and caused Judah to sin more than the surrounding nations. Yet even Manasseh is in the Messianic genealogy. The inclusion of faithless kings in the line from David to Christ demonstrates that the Messianic promise operated through divine faithfulness to the covenant, not through the worthiness of its bearers. Paul’s statement ‘if we are faithless, he remains faithful — for he cannot deny himself’ (2 Timothy 2:13) describes the very covenant logic that kept the Davidic line intact through Manasseh.
15: Josiah’s sons: Johanan (firstborn, apparently died before ruling), Jehoiakim (second son, ruled after Jehoahaz/Shallum), Zedekiah (installed as king after Jeconiah), and Shallum (= Jehoahaz, ruled 3 months). Matthew 1:11: ‘Josiah was the father of Jechoniah and his brothers, at the time of the deportation to Babylon.’ Matthew’s ‘brothers’ of Jechoniah likely refers to Josiah’s other sons who were alive at the deportation, reflecting the political complexity of this period (three of Josiah’s sons briefly ruled: Jehoahaz, Jehoiakim, Zedekiah). Josiah himself (2 Chr 34-35) was the great covenant-reforming king whose death at Megiddo shocked the nation; his reign was the last Davidic high point before the Babylonian destruction.
16: Jehoiakim’s sons: Jeconiah (Coniah, Jehoiachin) and Zedekiah. Jeconiah is the genealogically critical figure: Jeremiah 22:30 declared him ‘childless... for none of his offspring shall succeed in sitting on the throne of David and ruling again in Judah.’ Yet 1 Chronicles 3:17-18 lists Jeconiah’s seven sons, and Matthew 1:12 continues the genealogy through Jeconiah. The resolution: the Matthean genealogy (through Joseph) carries the legal-Davidic succession through Jeconiah, giving Jesus the legal right to the Davidic throne through Joseph’s adoption. But Jesus’s biological descent (through Mary, Luke 3) goes through Nathan, not Solomon — bypassing Jeconiah entirely. The virgin birth simultaneously maintains the legal Davidic claim (through Joseph/Jeconiah’s line) and avoids the biological application of Jeremiah’s curse. Haggai 2:23 anticipates the reversal: YHWH calls Zerubbabel (Jeconiah’s grandson) his ‘signet ring’ (ḥôtām) — the same image YHWH removed from Coniah (Jeremiah 22:24) — pointing toward ultimate restoration of the Davidic line.
17: Sons of Jeconiah (the captive): Shealtiel his firstborn, Malchiram, Pedaiah, Shenazzar, Jekamiah, Hoshama, and Nedabiah. Shealtiel appears in both Matthew 1:12 and Luke 3:27 as the post-exilic Davidic link. The title ‘Jeconiah the captive’ (yᵉḵonyāh hāʾăsîr) marks his exile status; the Davidic line continued in Babylonian captivity, preserved even in exile. The preservation of the royal genealogy through the Babylonian exile is the genealogical parallel to the preservation of the covenant promise through the death and resurrection: the Davidic line appeared to end at the deportation just as the Messianic hope appeared to end at the cross, but both were preserved through and beyond their nadir.
18: Malchiram, Pedaiah, and Shenazzar among Jeconiah’s sons. Shenazzar may be identified with the Sheshbazzar of Ezra 1:8-11, the ‘prince of Judah’ who led the first return from exile and laid the foundation of the second temple. If Shenazzar = Sheshbazzar, then the temple foundation was laid by a son of Jeconiah — the first restoration act performed by the generation directly after the curse, suggesting the curse’s limitation was being worked out even in the first returnees.
19: Pedaiah’s sons: Zerubbabel and Shimei. Both Matthew 1:12-13 and Luke 3:27 include Zerubbabel as the post-exilic Davidic heir. Haggai 2:23 is the decisive Christological text for Zerubbabel: ‘On that day, declares YHWH of hosts, I will take you, O Zerubbabel my servant, the son of Shealtiel, and make you like a signet ring (ḥôtām), for I have chosen you.’ YHWH removed the signet from Coniah (Jeremiah 22:24: ‘though Coniah... were the signet ring on my right hand, yet I would tear you off’); he gives it back to Zerubbabel. Zechariah’s vision of Zerubbabel completing the temple by ‘not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit’ (Zechariah 4:6) became the defining prophetic paradigm for the Messiah’s spiritual kingdom-building (John 2:19-21; 2 Corinthians 6:16; 1 Peter 2:5).
20: Zerubbabel’s children: Meshullam, Hananiah, Shelomith (their sister), Hashubah, Ohel, Berechiah, Hasadiah, and Jushab-hesed. The Davidic line continues for two generations beyond Zerubbabel in the Chronicles list. Zerubbabel’s son Meshullam and daughter Shelomith appear in Ezra-Nehemiah contexts (the Messianic family is embedded in the post-exilic restoration community). The Davidic line is now a royal family without a throne — a remnant preserved against the day of its restoration.
21: Hananiah’s sons: Pelatiah and Jeshaiah; and the sons of Rephaiah, of Arnan, of Obadiah, of Shecaniah. The post-exilic Davidic genealogy continues. Shecaniah appears in Nehemiah 3:29 and Ezra 8:3 as a post-exilic leader. The Davidic family is integrated into the broader post-exilic restoration community — not a royal court but a covenant family awaiting the fulfillment of the dynastic promise.
22: Sons of Shecaniah: Shemaiah; and sons of Shemaiah: Hattush, Igal, Bariah, Neariah, and Shaphat — six. Hattush son of Shecaniah returned from Babylon with Ezra (Ezra 8:2-3). The post-exilic Davidic family is present in the return-from-exile community — the genealogical line of the Messiah was among the first returnees to Judah, embedded in the very restoration that the prophets had predicted as Messianic preparation.
23: Sons of Neariah: Elioenai, Hizkiah, and Azrikam — three. The post-exilic Davidic line in its fifth generation after Zerubbabel. The Chronicles genealogy extends further than Matthew’s post-Zerubbabel list (Matthew 1:13-16: Abiud → Eliakim → Azor → Zadok → Achim → Eliud → Eleazar → Matthan → Jacob → Joseph — nine generations from Zerubbabel to Joseph). Matthew’s list may trace a different sub-branch of the Davidic family than Chronicles’, or use a different counting convention, or selectively compress — the two lists are genealogical and not strictly sequential records.
24: Sons of Elioenai: Hodaviah, Eliashib, Pelaiah, Akkub, Johanan, Delaiah, and Anani — seven. The Chronicles genealogy of the Davidic line ends here, approximately six or seven generations after Zerubbabel, placing its composition in the late 5th century BCE. ‘Anani’ in 3:24 has been connected by some interpreters to the Aramaic title ‘the one who is coming’ (ʿănānî ← ʿānān, cloud), reading the final name of the Davidic genealogy as a veiled Messianic reference (cf. Daniel 7:13: ‘one like a son of man, coming with the clouds (ʿănānê) of heaven’). Whether or not this is intentional, the post-exilic Davidic genealogy ends here — and the line it traces runs from Adam (1 Chronicles 1:1) through Abraham, Judah, David, the kings, the exile, Zerubbabel’s restoration, and the post-exilic community all the way to the generation in which Matthew’s genealogy picks up the thread and carries it to Joseph and the birth of Jesus (Matthew 1:16).
